title: Classical Ciphers and Cryptanalysis as Defect Labs · Runtime Contract: Crypto Go Legacy Migration Boundary
summary: A training tool presents Caesar, substitution, repeating-key XOR, and one-time pads as equivalent encryption choices and accidentally ships the toy implementation. Predict the asset, adversary, security property, trust boundary, and falsifying evidence before opening the governing standard. Planned artifact: Add and verify the legacy migration boundary increment in classical cipher attack and migration dossier. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal…
objectives: Quarantine toy and obsolete algorithms behind fixtures, negative tests, decryption-only migration, telemetry, deadlines, and deletion criteria.
Model offline and online search by keyspace, guess rate, parallelism, detection, lockout, value, and uncertainty without attacking unauthorized targets.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Cryptography and Go Predict Crypto Go Legacy Migration Boundary · Theory Crypto Go Legacy Migration…
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory cryptographic engineering team handles case 4457 during a signed release review. Build deterministic Go evidence to model an authorized classical-cipher attack while quarantining all legacy use to migration or defect labs; keep browser execution deterministic and move production entropy, native timing, non-allowlisted crypto, network, HSM, and compliance claims to authorized transfer gates; then rotate the active key while old data remains readable and new writes cannot downgrade. Browser code uses pure state models or the narrow side-effect-free crypto allowlist with fixed fixtures. Production entropy and keys, secret custody, native timing and side channels, networks, TLS, HSM/KMS, certificate systems, FIPS or compliance state, load, faults, restore, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Quarantine toy and obsolete algorithms behind fixtures, negative tests, decryption-only migration, telemetry, deadlines, and deletion criteria.
